text: This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on circulating-fuel reactors and all other ANP research at the Laboratory under its Contract W-7405-eng-26.
The ANP Project is comprised of about 400 technical and scientific personnel engaged in many phases of research directed toward the achievement of nuclear propulsion of aircraft.
The ART is to be a power plant system that will include a 60-Mw circulating-fuel reflector-moderated reactor and adequate means for heat disposal.

The design work, as well as the supporting research on materials and problems peculiar to the ART (previously included in the subject sections), is now reported as a subsection of Part I, Section 2, "Reflector-Moderated Reactor."

The apparent melting point of the mixture so obtained was $630^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A filtrate obtained at $645^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ showed 3.9 and $4.0\%$ $\mathsf{U}^{3+}$ by two different analytical methods and $4.66\%$ total uranium.

Since $\mathsf{UF}_4$ is quite soluble in this melt the insoluble uranium compound is certainly trivalent; about $95\%$ of the $\mathsf{UF}_4$ charged was reduced to $\mathsf{UF}_3$ ; about $85\%$ of the uranium in the filtrate was trivalent.

Five thousand pounds of low-hafnium $\mathsf{ZrCl}_4$ was received for conversion into $\mathsf{ZrF_4}$ at Building 9211 in Y-12.
Approximately $12\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ was purified by hydrofluorination at $600^{\circ}C$ to meet the requirements for the preparation of $\mathsf{UF}_3$ and various experimental uses.
Three batches of $\mathsf{FeF}_3$ were prepared by hydrofluorination of anhydrous $\mathsf{FeCl}_3$ at $450^{\circ}C$ followed by a helium flush at this temperature.

Approximately 8 lb of $(\mathsf{NH}_4)_3\mathsf{CrF}_6$ was synthesized by heating an excess of $\mathsf{NH}_4\mathsf{HF}_2$ with $\mathsf{CrF}_3\cdot 3\frac{1}{3}\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ at $200^{\circ}C$ .

A portion of the $(\mathsf{NH}_4)_3\mathsf{CrF}_6$ was thermally decomposed under helium at $700^{\circ}C$ to yield $\mathsf{CrF}_3$ , and another portion, after being decomposed to $\mathsf{CrF}_3$ at $600^{\circ}C$ , was reduced to $\mathsf{CrF}_2$ by heating under hydrogen at $800^{\circ}C$ .

Approximately 4 lb of $\mathsf{LaF}_3$ was prepared from $\mathsf{La}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ by converting the oxide to an aqueous solution of $\mathsf{LaCl}_3$ , precipitating the fluoride by decantation and centrifugation, and finally drying at $150^{\circ}C$ in a silver vessel.

Approximate absorption spectra have been measured for fused sodium hydroxide at various temperatures up to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in air with sodium hydroxide at $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ as a reference.
Limitations of the instrument restricted measurements to the wave length range 400 to $650\mu$ .

The experimental procedure consisted briefly of placing a periclase (magnesium oxide crystal) cell containing sodium hydroxide in the light path and at a given temperature measuring the photocell response at various wave lengths relative to the response at $600\mathrm{m}\mu$ which is the wave length of maximum response at $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Repetition of this procedure at various temperatures gave a series of curves that intersected at $600\mathrm{m}\mu$ and, by their general form, gave some indication of how the absorption was changing with temperature.

With increasing temperature a rapid increase in absorption appears at the shorter wave lengths, until, at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the absorption at $400\mathrm{m}\mu$ reverses the trend and drops markedly; however, at $440\mathrm{m}\mu$ , the absorption again increases and results in a sharp absorption peak at $440\mathrm{m}\mu$ .
